query: is non-HDL-cholesterol a better predictor of mortality than LDL-cholesterol in patients following an acute myocardial infarction

retrieved_docs.title: Low HDL Cholesterol -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: According to the Framingham Heart Study, the risk of myocardial infarction increases by approximately 25% for every 5 mg/dL decrease in HDL cholesterol serum level below median values. [31] Furthermore, a low HDL cholesterol level is an independent risk factor for myocardial infarction in patients with established cardiovascular disease. Moreover, the SMART (Secondary Manifestations of Arterial Disease) study demonstrated that elevated HDL cholesterol is protective against future cardiac events in patients on statin therapy. Thus, patients have a better cardiovascular prognosis when HDL cholesterol levels are higher. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Hypoalphalipoproteinemia -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The Lipid Research Clinics Prevalence Mortality Follow-up Study (LRCF) documented only fatal outcomes. A 1-mg/dL increase in HDL cholesterol decreased cardiovascular disease mortality rates by 3.7% in men and 4.7% in women. [38]

retrieved_docs.title: Non–ST-Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Patients who present with NSTEMI have a lower 6-month mortality rate than those who present with unstable angina. Morbidity and mortality further depend on the degree of troponin elevation as well as comorbid conditions such as the severity of diabetes, presence of peripheral vascular disease, presence of renal dysfunction, and dementia. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Hollenhorst Plaque -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with retinal cholesterol plaques had a higher incidence of stroke compared to their healthy counterparts (8.5% vs 0.8%). [12]  Cholesterol emboli have a 15% risk of mortality at 1 year, 29% at 3 years, and 54% at 7 years. [12] Hollenhorst observed that survival reduced by almost 15% and 40% at 1 and 8 years, respectively, in patients with retinal cholesterol embolism. [4] These patients have increased odds of stroke-related deaths (hazard ratio, 2.61). [9] The cumulative mortality was 56% with retinal emboli and 30% without retinal emboli. [4] The risk of nonfatal myocardial infarction or vascular death was 7.7% (4.9% among controls). [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Complex Coronary Artery Lesions -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Complex coronary lesions carry higher overall mortality in patients with stable angina than those with noncomplex lesions. These complex lesions are often associated with diabetes, lower ventricular ejection fraction, and lower HDL-C. The SYNTAX Score and SYNTAX Score II are frequently the means to decide on the best revascularization approach. These scoring systems are widely used and have been validated in many studies. [52] [53] [54] Studies have shown SYNTAX Score II to be superior to the SYNTAX score as a prognostic tool.

retrieved_docs.title: Unstable Angina -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The atherogenic index of plasma, calculated as the logarithm (base 10) of the ratio of triglycerides to high-density lipoprotein cholesterol, serves as a novel biomarker for assessing the risk of coronary heart disease and atherosclerosis. [29] In patients with prediabetes, the atherogenic index of plasma demonstrates a significant correlation with the prognosis of unstable angina. [30] Additionally, exposure to specific air pollutants, particularly carbon monoxide and particulate matter with a diameter of 10 µm or less, has been associated with an increased risk of heart failure readmissions among patients with unstable angina. [31]

retrieved_docs.content: Sex Age Family history Male pattern baldness

retrieved_docs.title: Non–ST-Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The outcomes of patients with NSTEMI depend on the severity of the myocardial injury, compliance with treatment and other comorbidities. Patients who do not change their risk factors for the coronary disease have a poor outcome. [15] [16] (Level V)

retrieved_docs.title: Non–ST-Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: When NSTEMI has been diagnosed, patients should be admitted to cardiac care units for further management. Beta-blocker therapy should be started within 24 hours after the presentation in patients who do not have a contraindication. Unless otherwise contraindicated, ACE Inhibitors should be initiated in patients with an ejection fraction of less than 40%, hypertension, diabetes, or chronic kidney disease. High-dose statins should be initiated for cholesterol management. Invasive and non-invasive testing strategies are employed. Both early intervention strategies with diagnostic angiography and intervention are applied as indicated, and conservative medical management strategies are employed.

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Artery Disease -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: The MIRACL trial investigated statin use. Patients with diabetes or low HDL cholesterol are at a higher risk and gain more significant absolute benefit from early treatment with 80 mg of atorvastatin daily compared to those without these risk factors. In the overall population of the MIRACL study, administering 80 mg of atorvastatin resulted in a 2.6% reduction in the absolute risk of experiencing a primary endpoint event, such as death, nonfatal myocardial infarction, resuscitated cardiac arrest, and recurrent ischemia, compared to placebo. For patients with diabetes or low HDL cholesterol, the reduction in absolute risk was even more significant, at 4.4% and 3.7%, respectively. [55]

retrieved_docs.title: Cholesterol Levels -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Primary prevention recommendations for adults aged 40 to 75 with an LDL-C level of 70 to 189 mg/dL focus on managing high LDL-C and low HDL-C levels, which are risk factors for coronary heart disease. [22] [23] Large clinical trials have demonstrated that lowering LDL-C levels significantly reduces cardiovascular events and mortality rates. However, current consensus guidelines do not recommend targeting HDL-C for primary prevention of coronary artery disease. [24] Nevertheless, recent studies have suggested that serum cholesterol efflux capacity may have prognostic value in patients with CAD. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Pleurisy -- Prognosis -- Cardiovascular Conditions
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction has a mortality rate of 30%, with nearly 50% of deaths occurring before patients reach the hospital. Early reperfusion through fibrinolysis or percutaneous coronary intervention within 90 minutes of arrival, along with preserved left ventricular function and short- and long-term treatment with β-blockers, aspirin, and angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, all contribute to improved survival. The strongest predictor of a poor outcome is poorly preserved left ventricular function. [50] Additional factors that worsen mortality include: Older age Diabetes Delayed reperfusion Elevated B-type natriuretic peptide and high-sensitivity C-reactive protein Depression Involvement of lead aVR on ECG [51] Presence of heart failure or pulmonary edema History of cerebrovascular disease or peripheral vascular disease Elevated thrombolysis in myocardial infarction risk score for unstable angina/non–ST-elevation acute coronary syndrome

retrieved_docs.title: Familial Hypercholesterolemia, Type 2A(Archived) -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis of patients with familial hypercholesterolemia is worse than those without this disease at any level of untreated low-density lipoprotein cholesterol. Patients with acute coronary syndrome and a history of heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia treated with high dose statins have three times the highest risk of death and nonfatal myocardial infarction within one year when compared to matched individuals without familial hypercholesterolemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperlipidemia -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Adults with ongoing exposure to moderate or severe elevations in non-HDL cholesterol levels have concurrent elevated risks for developing coronary heart disease and would likely benefit from aggressive medical treatment modalities, including, which includes high-intensity statin therapy in addition to diet and lifestyle modifications. Based on a 20 year follow up of the "West of Scotland Coronary Prevention Study," the researchers noted that patients who received statin therapy for five years had improved survival rates and a clinically significant reduction in cardiovascular disease over the 20 years. This data supports the numerous other studies and trials that also reveal a significant cardiovascular risk reduction when using statin therapy appropriately, and there was the implementation of a proactive treatment approach. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Myocardial Infarction -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Medical management following an AMI is crucial for improving long-term outcomes. Initiating medications, such as aspirin, beta-blockers, and ACE inhibitors, is standard practice to help prevent recurrent cardiovascular events. Factors that negatively affect prognosis include the following: Diabetes Advanced age Prior MI, peripheral vascular disease, or stroke Delayed reperfusion Diminished ejection fraction (the strongest predictor) Presence of congestive heart failure Elevated C-reactive protein and BNP levels Depression

retrieved_docs.title: Low HDL Cholesterol -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: HDL levels are generally inversely proportional to cardiovascular disease risk. However, the clinical correlation between HDL cholesterol elevation and cardiac disease is incompletely understood. While results from several studies have shown that higher HDL levels are associated with a lower risk of cardiovascular disease, a 2009 meta-analysis failed to find this association after adjusting for changes in LDL cholesterol. [21] Furthermore, results from a 2022 cohort study demonstrated that very high HDL cholesterol levels are paradoxically associated with higher mortality in patients with coronary artery disease. Thus, the need to treat HDL levels is unclear and is not currently a standard of care in cholesterol management. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: Unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة) and chest pain (ألم الصدر) are both clinically associated with hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم). This relationship indicates that elevated blood cholesterol levels can contribute to the development of acute coronary syndromes such as unstable angina and may manifest as chest discomfort. Recognizing hypercholesterolemia as an underlying factor for these cardiac presentations underscores the importance of lipid screening and management in patients who exhibit chest pain or anginal symptoms, thereby aiding in risk stratification and preventive cardiovascular care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2830
retrieved_docs.content: Hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم) is clinically associated with unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة) and with both type 1 diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري نوع 1) and type 2 diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري نوع 2). This interrelationship indicates that elevated blood cholesterol levels contribute to the development of acute coronary syndromes such as unstable angina and are commonly observed in patients with either form of diabetes. Consequently, careful monitoring and management of lipid levels are essential components of care for individuals with diabetes to mitigate cardiovascular risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2829
retrieved_docs.content: Hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم) is clinically associated with unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة), acute pancreatitis (الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياسِ الحادّ), and chest pain (ألم الصَدْر). These connections indicate that elevated cholesterol levels can contribute to cardiovascular manifestations such as unstable angina and chest pain, while also being linked to inflammatory conditions like acute pancreatitis. Recognizing these relationships supports a more comprehensive evaluation and management strategy for patients with high cholesterol.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1085
retrieved_docs.content: Atorvastatin, fluvastatin and rosuvastatin are all members of the class of HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This drug class works by inhibiting the HMG CoA reductase enzyme, the rate‑limiting step in hepatic cholesterol synthesis, leading to reduced low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels and decreased risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. As plain lipid‑modifying agents, these statins are commonly prescribed for the treatment of hyperlipidaemia and for primary and secondary prevention of cardiovascular events. While they share the same mechanism of action, each agent differs in potency, pharmacokinetic properties and individual safety profiles, allowing clinicians to tailor therapy to the specific needs of each patient.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2830
retrieved_docs.content: Chest pain (ألم الصَدْر) is clinically associated with hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم). Elevated blood cholesterol promotes atherosclerotic plaque development in coronary arteries, which can impair myocardial blood flow and present as chest pain. Recognizing this link enables clinicians to assess patients with chest pain for underlying lipid abnormalities and to consider lipid‑lowering interventions as part of comprehensive cardiovascular risk management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2841
retrieved_docs.content: Unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة) and acute pancreatitis (الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياسِ الحادّ) are both clinically associated with hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم). Hypercholesterolemia, defined by elevated blood cholesterol levels, is a recognized risk factor for atherosclerotic disease that can precipitate unstable angina, and it may also contribute to inflammatory conditions such as acute pancreatitis. Recognizing these connections highlights the importance of effective lipid management to reduce the risk of both cardiovascular events and pancreatic inflammation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2462
retrieved_docs.content: HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid modifying drugs constitute a therapeutic class of statin medications that lower cholesterol by inhibiting the HMG‑CoA reductase enzyme in the hepatic cholesterol synthesis pathway. Atorvastatin, fluvastatin, and pravastatin are individual agents that belong to this class. By reducing low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and modestly increasing high‑density lipoprotein (HDL), these drugs are employed to manage hyperlipidemia and to lower the risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Their shared mechanism of action and lipid‑modifying effects make them central components of preventive cardiology and patient care strategies aimed at controlling dyslipidemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 779
retrieved_docs.content: Atorvastatin, fluvastatin, rosuvastatin, and cerivastatin are all members of the class HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This drug class works by inhibiting the enzyme HMG‑CoA reductase, the rate‑limiting step in cholesterol biosynthesis, which leads to reductions in low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and triglycerides and modest increases in high‑density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol. As plain lipid‑modifying agents, these statins are widely used to treat hyperlipidemia and to lower the risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Recognizing that atorvastatin, fluvastatin, rosuvastatin, and cerivastatin belong to the same pharmacologic group aids clinicians in selecting appropriate therapy, anticipating class‑related benefits, and monitoring for shared adverse effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1001
retrieved_docs.content: Atorvastatin, fluvastatin, and cerivastatin are all members of the class of HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, which are classified as plain lipid‑modifying drugs. As HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, these agents act by blocking the rate‑limiting enzyme in cholesterol biosynthesis, leading to reduced low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels and a lower risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Their inclusion in the plain lipid‑modifying drug category underscores their primary role in managing hyperlipidemia and preventing related cardiovascular events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1191
retrieved_docs.content: Fluvastatin, rosuvastatin, and cerivastatin are all members of the class of HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. As HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, these agents act by blocking the rate‑limiting enzyme in cholesterol biosynthesis, leading to reduced low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels and a lower risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Their classification as plain lipid‑modifying drugs underscores their primary therapeutic role in managing dyslipidaemia without additional pharmacologic actions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1679
retrieved_docs.content: 4‑aminopyrazolo(3,4‑d)pyrimidine, also known by the name isa, is classified as an anticholesteremic agent; it is used to lower cholesterol levels in patients, contributing to the management of dyslipidemia and associated cardiovascular risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 1225
retrieved_docs.content: Fluvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin are all classified as members of the therapeutic group “HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs.” This class of agents inhibits the enzyme HMG CoA reductase, thereby reducing hepatic cholesterol synthesis and lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels. As plain lipid‑modifying drugs, these statins are used to manage hyperlipidaemia and reduce cardiovascular risk in patients with elevated cholesterol. Recognizing that fluvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin share this mechanism of action and therapeutic category helps clinicians select appropriate statin therapy based on individual drug properties, efficacy, and safety profiles.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2742
retrieved_docs.content: فرط كوليسترول الدم (hypercholesterolemia) is clinically associated with مرض السكري نوع 1 (type 1 diabetes mellitus), indicating that elevated cholesterol levels frequently coexist with this form of diabetes. In addition, ألم الصَدْر (chest pain) is clinically associated with فرط كوليسترول الدم, suggesting that patients who have high cholesterol may present with chest discomfort, which can reflect underlying cardiovascular involvement. The combined relationships imply that individuals with مرض السكري نوع 1 who also develop فرط كوليسترول الدم are at heightened risk for experiencing ألم الصَدْر and related cardiac complications, underscoring the importance of regular lipid monitoring and vigilant assessment of chest pain symptoms in the management of type 1 diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1973
retrieved_docs.content: Lipid modifying agents are classified as a subset of cardiovascular system drugs, meaning they belong to the broader group of medications used to treat conditions of the cardiovascular system. As cardiovascular system drugs, lipid modifying agents are employed to manage abnormal lipid levels, lower cholesterol, and reduce the risk of atherosclerotic disease, thereby contributing to overall cardiovascular health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1282
retrieved_docs.content: Fluvastatin and pravastatin are both classified as members of the HMG CoA reductase inhibitor group of plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This drug class works by inhibiting the HMG‑CoA reductase enzyme, thereby reducing hepatic cholesterol synthesis and lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels in patients. As members of the same therapeutic category, fluvastatin and pravastatin share a common mechanism of action and are used to manage hyperlipidemia and reduce cardiovascular risk. Recognizing that both agents belong to the HMG CoA reductase inhibitor, plain lipid‑modifying drug class helps clinicians select appropriate statin therapy based on individual patient factors while understanding their shared pharmacologic properties.
